This approach minimizes the time you spend feeling depleted, and you can recover your energy by immediately rehydrating with an electrolyte drink after you step off the scale.

I'm training for a marathon, and I keep getting conflicting advice about pain. Some people say "no pain, no gain," and others say to stop if anything hurts. I get a lot of aches and soreness after my long runs. How do I know when to push through and when to back off?

A good general rule is to pay attention to how the pain behaves during the first mile of your run. If an ache or soreness lessens as you warm up and settles into the background, it's usually the type of general fatigue that you can work through. The pains to be concerned about are the ones that are sharp, specific, or get noticeably worse as you continue to run, which are clear signals to stop.

My climbing partner and I are starting to lead trad routes. We bought a full new rack of cams and nuts. How important is it to place gear on every pitch of an easy, low-angle climb? It feels like overkill and slows us down when the climbing itself feels like a scramble.

On very easy, low-angle terrain where you feel completely solid, it's reasonable to place minimal gear or even move together without placing any. The primary purpose of protection is to arrest a dangerous fall, and if the climbing feels more like scrambling, placing gear every few feet can be unnecessary and counterproductive to your flow. Focus on identifying specific steps or sections where a slip could actually lead to injury and protect those, rather than treating every pitch as a gear-placement drill.

I'm going canyoneering for the first time with some friends who have been a few times before. They say all I need are good shoes and a harness, and I can just use their ropes and gear. Is that a reasonable way to get started?

That's a very common way for people to get introduced to the sport. As long as you trust your friends and have a harness that fits you correctly, using shared team gear like ropes is standard practice. The one piece of personal gear I would absolutely add to your list is a helmet, as it's essential protection that everyone in the canyon needs to wear.

I'm going canyoneering with some friends. We're pretty experienced. I see a lot of people building complex, redundant anchors, but sometimes there's a perfectly solid-looking tree or rock horn right at the drop. Is it overkill to always build a full anchor if a natural feature looks bomber?

When you encounter a truly bomber natural anchor, like a thick, healthy tree or a massive, solid rock horn, building a complex redundant system is often unnecessary. The primary reason for multi-point anchors is to create collective strength from individually weaker placements. If your team thoroughly inspects a single feature and agrees it is unquestionably solid, using it directly is an efficient and common practice.

My friends and I want to go canyoneering this weekend. The forecast for the canyon area itself is clear, but there's a 20% chance of "isolated thunderstorms" in the mountains about 30 miles upstream. Is it being paranoid to cancel the trip for such a small, distant chance of rain?

Canceling for a 20% chance of isolated storms that far upstream would be a very conservative call. Many experienced canyoneers would proceed with that forecast, as the likelihood of a significant event reaching you is quite low. The practical approach is to go, but keep an efficient pace and stay aware of conditions throughout the day.

I'm getting back into bench pressing after a minor rotator cuff strain. My physical therapist said to keep the weight light, but I feel like that's not doing anything. When I use a heavier weight, I feel a slight "twinge" of pain, but it's not sharp or unbearable. Should I listen to that signal and back off, or is it okay to push through minor discomfort?

A slight twinge is often different from the sharp pain of an actual injury, and learning to work through that minor discomfort is part of getting stronger again. As long as the sensation remains a minor "twinge" and doesn't become sharp or last long after your set, it's generally an indication that you're challenging the muscle appropriately. Pay close attention to your form, and you can likely continue working with that heavier weight to progressively rebuild your strength.

I'm an intermediate sea kayaker and want to do a 10-mile coastal paddle this weekend. The forecast is for 15-knot winds and 2-3 foot waves. It's sunny, but those conditions are at the edge of my comfort zone. Should I just wait for a calmer day?

This sounds like a great opportunity to expand your skills, as pushing your boundaries is key to progression. Those conditions are a classic next step for an intermediate paddler, and a 10-mile trip is a reasonable way to test yourself. If your self-rescue is solid, you should view this as a challenge to embrace rather than an unnecessary risk.

I’m trying to get better at whitewater kayaking. I feel like getting out of my boat to scout every rapid really slows me down and kills the flow of the trip. Is it better to just learn to "read and run" from the cockpit?

Yes, learning to read and run is a crucial skill for improving your whitewater paddling and building confidence. While scouting has its place, over-relying on it can become a crutch that slows your development of the on-the-fly decision-making that defines fluid paddling. If you can see the main current and the general character of the rapid from your boat, it's often better to trust your judgment and run it, as this is the fastest way to learn to read water effectively.

I'm going on a high-altitude trek soon (around 15,000 feet) and I live at sea level. I only have a day or two to acclimatize on-site. I've read about Acute Mountain Sickness. How serious are the initial symptoms like a headache or mild nausea?

Mild symptoms like a headache or slight nausea are very common and are often just a sign that your body is beginning to adjust to the altitude. It is generally fine to continue with your trek as long as you pay close attention to how you feel and the symptoms remain mild. Many trekkers find these initial feelings pass as they keep moving, so just be sure to stay well-hydrated and consider stopping only if the symptoms start to worsen significantly.

I'm planning a canyoneering trip in Utah. The weather forecast for the day has a 20% chance of thunderstorms in the late afternoon. Is that a low enough risk to go for it if we start early and move fast?

A 20% chance of thunderstorms, especially for the late afternoon, is a common scenario that can often be managed with a solid plan. If you get an early start and are confident in your group's ability to move efficiently, you can reasonably expect to be out of the canyon before that weather window becomes a serious factor. The key is simply to execute your plan and be well clear of the main drainage by early afternoon.
I want to get into whitewater kayaking, but every time I watch videos, it looks so chaotic. I can't tell where the safe lines are. How do people learn to read the water and not just panic?

The best way to start reading water is to get on a simple, forgiving stretch and follow an experienced paddler. By watching their line and feeling how your boat reacts to the currents they navigate, you’ll quickly build an intuitive sense of where the water wants to push you. You'll find that what looks like chaos from a video is actually just a few repeating patterns that your boat will teach you how to handle through direct experience.
